# How to Connect DeepSeek V4 to Codex

A practical CC Switch guide for connecting DeepSeek V4 to Codex, with a focus on local routing, model checks, and common 404 issues.

If you use `Codex` as part of your daily coding workflow and want to switch the upstream model to `DeepSeek V4`, the cleanest path is usually not editing `~/.codex/config.toml` by hand. It is letting `CC Switch` manage the provider and routing layer for you.

That approach helps in a few practical ways:

- you avoid hand-editing Codex config every time
- it is easier to tell whether the active provider really changed
- routing issues are easier to separate from provider issues

This guide focuses on one thing: `how to connect DeepSeek V4 to Codex and confirm that the route is actually working.`

## Prepare These First

Before you start, make sure you already have:

- `Codex`installed
- `CC Switch`installed and running
- a working `DeepSeek API Key`

It is also best to launch `Codex` at least once before doing the setup. That gives Codex a chance to create its local config structure, which makes provider takeover more predictable.

## Why Local Routing Matters for Codex

This is the part that is easiest to miss.

Current `Codex` workflows are built around the `OpenAI Responses API`, while `DeepSeek` commonly exposes an `OpenAI Chat Completions` compatible interface. Those two protocols are similar in spirit, but they do not use the same request body, stream format, or endpoint shape.

If you point Codex straight at a Chat Completions upstream, common problems include:

- the wrong model list
- `404`or- `400`errors
- streaming output that Codex cannot parse correctly

That is why `CC Switch` local routing is usually the key part of this setup. Codex continues to talk in the Responses format, CC Switch rewrites the request for DeepSeek, and then converts the upstream response back into the format Codex expects.

For `Codex + DeepSeek`, local routing is usually not optional. It is part of the working path.

## Step 1: Open the Codex Panel

In `CC Switch`, choose `Codex` from the app switcher.

If you do not see it, go to:

```
Settings → General → App Visibility
```
and make sure `Codex` is not hidden.

## Step 2: Add DeepSeek as a Provider

Inside the Codex panel, click the `+` button to add a provider.

For a first setup, use the built-in `DeepSeek` preset. It usually already includes the right request base, model menu, default model behavior, and the routing requirement.

The normal path is:

- choose the `DeepSeek`preset
- enter your `DeepSeek API Key`
- confirm the default model
- save the provider

For most users, the best starting model is one of these:

- `deepseek-v4-flash`
- `deepseek-v4-pro`

Use `Flash` for everyday work and move to `Pro` when the task gets heavier.

## Step 3: Turn On Local Routing

Go to the routing settings and confirm both of these are enabled:

- the main local routing switch
- the `Codex`toggle under`Routing Enabled`

The usual path is:

```
Settings → Routing → Local Routing
```
When routing is enabled, Codex is pointed to a local address, usually:

```
http://127.0.0.1:15721/v1
```
Your real DeepSeek key stays inside CC Switch, and the local route injects it when forwarding requests upstream.

## Step 4: Enable the Provider and Restart Codex

Return to the Codex provider list and click:

```
Enable
```
If the provider shows a `Needs Routing` style marker, that simply means the route must be running for that provider to work.

This is also where Codex differs from Claude Code: after switching providers, restarting the current Codex terminal session is the safer default.

That is usually because:

- Codex may have already loaded the old `config.toml`
- model catalog refresh often needs a fresh process

So the reliable habit is simple: `enable the provider, then restart Codex.`

## Step 5: Check the Model and Run a Small Test

Once Codex is back up, do two very small checks first:

- use `/model`to confirm the model is now coming from DeepSeek
- run one lightweight prompt and make sure it returns normally

A small validation task is enough, for example:

- explain a short code snippet
- summarize a small file
- rewrite a short requirement into a checklist

You are mainly confirming three things:

- Codex is really using DeepSeek now
- the model is the one you selected
- the response is not failing on auth, model naming, or protocol mismatch

## When to Suspect a Routing Problem First

Start with routing checks if you see things like:

- `404`errors, especially around- `/responses`
- the provider is enabled but Codex still seems unchanged
- `/model`does not show the expected DeepSeek model
- stream output or response format looks obviously wrong

Most of the time, one of these three states is out of sync:

- the active provider under the Codex tab is not actually DeepSeek
- the local routing service is not running
- the `Codex`toggle under`Routing Enabled`is off

## Where the Config Usually Lives

If you need to troubleshoot later, the main file to know is:

```
~/.codex/config.toml
```
With routing takeover enabled, this is commonly pointed at the local route instead of your upstream provider directly.

If you see Codex pointing straight at a DeepSeek Chat endpoint, there is a good chance the CC Switch conversion path has been bypassed.

## Common Questions

### Why does Codex still return 404 after I added DeepSeek?

Usually because a Chat Completions upstream was pointed at Codex directly instead of going through CC Switch local routing.

### Why does `/model` not show DeepSeek?

Restart Codex first. The provider may be fine, but the running process may not have refreshed the model catalog yet.

### Routing is on, so why are requests still going to the wrong place?

Check all three states together:

- the active Codex provider is DeepSeek
- the local routing service is running
- the `Codex`routing toggle is enabled

### Can I run official OpenAI Codex access through the same local route?

That is generally not recommended. The routing layer is mainly intended for third-party providers, aggregators, or protocol-conversion scenarios like DeepSeek.

## Summary

The practical setup path for `DeepSeek V4` in `Codex` is:

- launch Codex once first
- add the `DeepSeek`preset in the`Codex`panel in CC Switch
- enter your `DeepSeek API Key`
- turn on local routing and enable `Codex`routing
- enable the provider
- restart Codex
- use `/model`and a small test prompt to validate the route

If you only want the shortest takeaway, it is this:

`for Codex, connecting DeepSeek is not just about adding a provider. The local routing layer is part of the actual setup.`

title: GitHub - mmm1h/deepseek-openai-proxy: OpenAI-compatible proxy for DeepSeek thinking models — works with Codex and any OpenAI client · GitHub
markdown:
---
title: GitHub - mmm1h/deepseek-openai-proxy: OpenAI-compatible proxy for DeepSeek thinking models — works with Codex and any OpenAI client
author: Mmm
url: https://github.com/mmm1h/deepseek-openai-proxy
hostname: github.com
description: OpenAI-compatible proxy for DeepSeek thinking models — works with Codex and any OpenAI client - mmm1h/deepseek-openai-proxy
sitename: GitHub
categories: ['repository:1222929073']
---
本项目是一个本地 OpenAI 兼容代理，主要给 Codex 使用 DeepSeek thinking 模型时兜底协议差异。

当前目标链路：

```
Codex
  => cli proxy api
  => deepseek-thinking-proxy (127.0.0.1:9000/v1)
  => DeepSeek /chat/completions
```
它同时保留 `/v1/chat/completions`，并提供 `/v1/responses`，让 Codex 0.125+ 可以使用 `wire_api = "responses"`。

- DeepSeek thinking tool-call 轮次要求后续请求带回上一轮 assistant 的 `reasoning_content`，Codex/OpenAI 兼容客户端通常不会带。代理会把 DeepSeek 返回的 reasoning 存到本地 SQLite，再在后续请求里补回去；冷缓存恢复默认只打日志，不污染回答。
- Codex 会发 OpenAI Responses 协议，DeepSeek 官方主接口是 Chat Completions。代理会把 `instructions`、`input`、function tools、Codex local shell/custom/apply_patch/MCP 调用记录和 Responses SSE 互相转换。
- Codex 内建 `web_search`是 OpenAI 平台托管工具，DeepSeek 不认识。代理可选接入 Brave Search MCP：把 Responses`web_search`或`bravesearch`/`brave_search`/`brave_web_search`alias 映射成内部搜索 function，DeepSeek 触发后由代理自己调用 Brave，再把搜索结果喂回 DeepSeek。
- `store:true`/- `previous_response_id`由本地 SQLite state shim 兜底，方便 Codex Plan Mode 这类 Responses stateful 形态继续跑；这不是 OpenAI 云端 conversation state。
- 普通 function tools 不会被代理吃掉，仍然回给 Codex 执行。

```
cd F:\git_pjt\deepseek-thinking-proxy
$env:DEEPSEEK_API_KEY = "你的 DeepSeek API Key"
uv run deepseek-openai-proxy --port 9000 --verbose
```
健康检查：

`curl.exe http://127.0.0.1:9000/v1/healthz`清空本地 reasoning cache：

`uv run deepseek-openai-proxy --clear-reasoning-cache`首次运行会创建：

- `config.yaml`
- `reasoning_content.sqlite3`

这两个文件默认放在项目根目录，比较直观；`config.yaml` 和 SQLite cache 都属于本地状态，不提交。

推荐 Codex provider：

```
model = "deepseek-v4-pro"
model_provider = "deepseek_proxy"
[model_providers.deepseek_proxy]
name = "DeepSeek via local proxy"
base_url = "http://127.0.0.1:9000/v1"
env_key = "DEEPSEEK_API_KEY"
wire_api = "responses"
```
如果你中间还有一层 `cli proxy api`，那一层应把 Codex 的 OpenAI/Responses 请求继续转发到：

```
http://127.0.0.1:9000/v1
```
不要把 `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` 写进仓库。放环境变量里就行。

默认策略是 `web_search=auto`：

- `config.yaml`里有- `brave_api_key`：代理启用内部 hosted web search。
- config 里为空，但设置了环境变量 `BRAVE_API_KEY`：也会启用。
- 两边都没有：代理丢弃 Responses `web_search`，避免 DeepSeek 因不认识 hosted tool 而 400；此时搜索不可用。

推荐直接写到项目根目录的 `config.yaml`：

```
brave_api_key: "你的 Brave Search API Key"
recovery_notice_mode: log
```
临时 PowerShell 环境变量也仍然可用：

```
$env:BRAVE_API_KEY = "你的 Brave Search API Key"
uv run deepseek-openai-proxy --port 9000 --verbose
```
实现细节：

- 代理通过官方 MCP Python SDK 启动 `npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-brave-search@latest`。
- 每次搜索最多 5 条结果。
- hosted tool loop 最多 3 轮。
- 单次 MCP 调用 20 秒超时。
- MCP 失败会作为简短 tool error 结果喂回 DeepSeek，不让 Codex SSE 协议直接断掉。
- 搜索策略保持中性：代理让搜索工具真实可见，但不会强行禁止模型选择普通 shell/curl。

```
uv run python -m unittest discover -s tests -v
uv run python scripts/lint-golden-principles.py
uv run python scripts/doc-garden.py
uv run --with pre-commit pre-commit run --all-files
```
真实 DeepSeek live 测试默认跳过，需要显式打开：

```
$env:RUN_LIVE_DEEPSEEK_TESTS = "1"
$env:LIVE_DEEPSEEK_KEY = $env:DEEPSEEK_API_KEY
uv run python -m unittest tests.test_live_deepseek_openai_proxy -v
```
- `/v1/responses`支持本地- `store:true`/- `previous_response_id`回放，但不实现 OpenAI 平台侧完整 conversation state。
- 图片、文件输入会被拒绝或降级为文本占位；DeepSeek chat-first 链路目前不做多模态转发。
- `web_search`不是 Codex 自己执行，而是代理通过 Brave MCP 托管执行。
- MCP 本轮只做协议兼容和调用记录转换，不让代理直接执行任意 MCP 写操作。
- 代理是单机个人使用取向，不做多用户鉴权、计费、限流或管理后台。

- Python 文件位于 `src/`、`tests/`、`scripts/`时，单文件不得超过 500 行；超过就先拆模块。
- 新功能优先复用现有 Responses adapter、chat transform、stream accumulator 和 reasoning store。
- 不新增第二套 reasoning cache。
- 不把 API key、搜索结果样例中的真实私钥、个人配置写进仓库。
- 任务完成且测试通过后，默认提交并同步到 GitHub，除非明确要求不要同步。

**L0/L1 Prefix Cache Optimizer for DeepSeek API — with API Format Adapters + Real-time Monitor Dashboard**

- **L0/L1 Cache Reordering**: Automatically reorders messages to maximize DeepSeek prefix cache hit rates (99%+ hit rate in production)
- **🔥 Cache Warmup**: Pre-heats cache on startup so the very first request already hits the cache
- **📄 Config File**:- `config.yaml`with environment variable overrides

Use DeepSeek from tools that weren't designed for it — the gateway translates between API protocols on the fly:

| Adapter | Endpoint | What It Does | 
|---|---|---|
| Responses API🟣 | `POST /v1/responses` | Lets Codex(`wire_api = "responses"`) use DeepSeek | 
| Anthropic Messages🤖 | `POST /v1/messages` | Lets Claude clientsuse DeepSeek | 
| OpenAI Chat✅ | `POST /v1/chat/completions` | Native format (built-in) | 

All adapters get **L0/L1 cache optimization**, **smart routing**, and **unified metrics**.

- **Real-time TUI Dashboard**: Beautiful terminal UI showing cache stats, hit rate trends, and adapter status
- **Web Admin Panel**🌐: Open- `http://localhost:8080/admin`in your browser — toggle adapters on/off at runtime, no restart needed

- **OpenAI-Compatible API**: Drop-in replacement for- `base_url`– just change one config line
- **Auto Key Decryption**: Automatically decrypts API keys from QwenPaw configuration
- **Smart Format Conversion**: Handles both OpenAI format and AgentScope block format transparently
- **One-Click Launch**: Single script to start gateway + monitor panel
- **🔗 Hermes Integration**: One-command auto-link script (- `link-hermes.py`)
- **🖥**: Compatible with OpenAI clients that check available models- `/v1/models`Endpoint

```
# 1. Edit config.yaml with your API key
# 2. Double-click this file:
启动Gateway.bat
```
```
# Install dependencies
pip install -e .
# Generate default config
python -m deepseek_gateway.launch --init-config
# Edit config.yaml → set your DEEPSEEK_API_KEY
# Launch with monitor dashboard
python -m deepseek_gateway.launch
# Launch with custom config
python -m deepseek_gateway.launch --config /path/to/config.yaml
# Gateway only (no monitor)
python -m deepseek_gateway.launch --no-monitor
# Custom port
python -m deepseek_gateway.launch --port 8081
```
```
from deepseek_gateway.gateway import app, init_gateway
from deepseek_gateway.config import load_config
import uvicorn
cfg = load_config()
init_gateway(cfg)
uvicorn.run(app, host=cfg.host, port=cfg.port)
```
Once the gateway is running, open your browser:

Toggle API adapters on/off at runtime — no restart needed.

The gateway can translate between different LLM API protocols, so tools built for other providers can seamlessly use DeepSeek.

| Protocol | Endpoint | Adapter | Typical Users | 
|---|---|---|---|
| OpenAI Chat Completions | `POST /v1/chat/completions` | ✅ Native | Most tools (Hermes, OpenRouter, etc.) | 
| OpenAI Responses API | `POST /v1/responses` | 🔄 Responses | Codex(`wire_api = "responses"`) | 
| Anthropic Messages | `POST /v1/messages` | 🤖 Anthropic | Claude CLI, Claude Code, Anthropic SDKs | 

```
                           ┌──────────────────────────┐
Codex ── POST /v1/responses │                          │
                           │   API Format Adapters     │
Claude ── POST /v1/messages │   ↕ Request/Response     │──── DeepSeek API
                           │     Conversion            │    (OpenAI format)
Other ── POST /v1/chat/     │                          │
         completions        │   + L0/L1 Reordering     │
                           │   + Smart Routing         │
                           │   + Cache Warmup          │
                           └──────────────────────────┘
```
Configure Codex++ to point to the gateway:

```
[model_providers.CodexPlusPlus]
wire_api = "responses"
base_url = "http://localhost:8080/v1"
experimental_bearer_token = "sk-your-deepseek-key"
```
Then Codex will send `POST /v1/responses` requests — the gateway translates them to DeepSeek Chat Completions, applies cache optimization, and translates responses back.

Point any Anthropic-compatible client to the gateway:

```
# Claude Code CLI
ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=http://localhost:8080 \
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-your-deepseek-key \
claude
# Or via config
export ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL="http://localhost:8080"
```

Note: Model names in requests (e.g.`"claude-sonnet-4-20250514"`) are handled by theRouter— configure routing rules in`config.yaml`to map them to DeepSeek models.

Toggle adapters on/off **without restarting** the gateway:

**Web Admin Panel** 🌐: Open http://localhost:8080/admin in your browser

**Or via API:**

```
# Disable Codex adapter
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/adapter/responses/disable
# Re-enable
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/adapter/responses/enable
# Check status
curl http://localhost:8080/adapter-status
```
```
adapters:
  responses: true      # POST /v1/responses (Codex)
  anthropic: true      # POST /v1/messages (Claude)
```
Or use environment variables:

```
export ADAPTER_RESPONSES_ENABLED=true
export ADAPTER_ANTHROPIC_ENABLED=true
```
Configuration priority: **Environment variables > config.yaml > Defaults**

```
gateway:
  host: 0.0.0.0
  port: 8080
deepseek:
  api_key: "sk-your-key-here"          # DEEPSEEK_API_KEY env var also works
  api_url: "https://api.deepseek.com/v1/chat/completions"
  model: "deepseek-v4-flash"
monitor:
  sample_rate: 1.0                     # 0.0–1.0 sampling rate
  refresh_interval: 2.0                # TUI refresh interval (seconds)
warmup:
  enabled: true                        # Preheat cache on startup 🔥
  system_prompt: ""                    # Custom warmup prompt (empty = default)
  interval: 240                        # Auto-refresh interval (sec), 0 = off
adapters:
  responses: true                      # POST /v1/responses (Codex) 🔌
  anthropic: true                      # POST /v1/messages (Claude) 🔌
daemon: false                          # Headless mode
```
| Variable | Override | 
|---|---|
| `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` | API key | 
| `GATEWAY_PORT` | Listen port | 
| `DEEPSEEK_API_URL` | Upstream URL | 
| `MONITOR_SAMPLE_RATE` | Sampling rate | 
| `WARMUP_ENABLED` | `true`/`false` | 
| `ADAPTER_RESPONSES_ENABLED` | Enable Responses API adapter | 
| `ADAPTER_ANTHROPIC_ENABLED` | Enable Anthropic Messages adapter | 

The gateway can pre-heat DeepSeek's prefix cache on startup and keep it alive:

- **Startup warmup**: Sends a minimal request immediately when the gateway starts
- **Cache keepalive**: Refreshes the cache every- `warmup_interval`seconds (default: 4 min)
- **Effect**: Even the- **first real request**gets ~70% cache hit rate instead of 0%

One command to link Hermes to the gateway:

```
# Auto-detect Hermes config and point DeepSeek provider to gateway
python link-hermes.py
# Preview changes without modifying
python link-hermes.py --dry-run
# Restore from backup
python link-hermes.py --restore
# Check status
python link-hermes.py --status
```
```
                           ┌───────────────────────────────┐
                           │     DeepSeek Cache Gateway    │
                           │                               │
  Codex ───── /v1/responses │  ┌─────────────────────┐      │
                           │  │ Responses Adapter    │      │
  Claude ──── /v1/messages │  ├─────────────────────┤      │────▶ DeepSeek API
                           │  │ Anthropic Adapter   │      │     (OpenAI format)
  Hermes ──── /v1/chat/    │  ├─────────────────────┤      │
              completions  │  │ Chat Completions    │      │
                           │  └──────────┬──────────┘      │
                           │             │                  │
                           │    ┌────────┴────────┐        │
                           │    │ L0/L1 Reordering │        │
                           │    ├─────────────────┤        │
                           │    │ Smart Router    │        │

                           │    │ Cache Warmup    │        │

                           │  └─────────────────────────┘  │
                           └───────────────────────────────┘
```
The gateway optimizes message order to maximize prefix cache hits:

- **L0 Layer (Stable Prefix)**: System messages + tool definitions → placed first, never changes
- **L1 Layer (Dynamic History)**: User/assistant conversation → ordered by relevance

**Before Reordering:**

```
[
  {"role": "user", "content": "What's 2+2?"},
  {"role": "assistant", "content": "4"},
  {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant"},  ← Unstable!
  {"role": "user", "content": "What's 3+3?"}
]
```
**After Reordering:**

```
[
  {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant"},  ← Stable L0
  {"role": "user", "content": "What's 2+2?"},
  {"role": "assistant", "content": "4"},
  {"role": "user", "content": "What's 3+3?"}
]
```
This ensures the system prompt (which is usually large and expensive) is always cached!

```
deepseek-cache-gateway/
├── config.yaml                      # Configuration file
├── link-hermes.py                   # One-command Hermes integration
├── 启动Gateway.bat                  # Windows one-click launcher
├── deepseek_gateway/
│   ├── __init__.py                  # Version: 0.3.0
│   ├── config.py                    # Configuration loader
│   ├── gateway.py                   # Core: FastAPI server + cache warmup + adapters
│   ├── monitor.py                   # Rich TUI dashboard
│   ├── launch.py                    # Unified launcher (auto-cleanup)
│   ├── reorder.py                   # L0/L1 cache optimization algorithm
│   ├── router.py                    # Smart routing engine
│   ├── cli.py                       # CLI entry point
│   └── adapters/                    # 🔌 API format adapters
│       ├── __init__.py              #   Registry + discovery
│       ├── base.py                  #   BaseAdapter abstract class
│       ├── responses.py             #   OpenAI Responses API ↔ DeepSeek
│       └── anthropic.py             #   Anthropic Messages API ↔ DeepSeek
```

title: GitHub - NewbieGameCoder/DeepSeekCache: DeepSeek Cache Hit Optimization · GitHub
markdown:
---
title: GitHub - NewbieGameCoder/DeepSeekCache: DeepSeek Cache Hit Optimization
author: NewbieGameCoder
url: https://github.com/NewbieGameCoder/DeepSeekCache
hostname: github.com
description: DeepSeek Cache Hit Optimization. Contribute to NewbieGameCoder/DeepSeekCache development by creating an account on GitHub.
sitename: GitHub
date: 2000-01-01
categories: ['repository:1252269731']
---
`dcache-proxy` ships the `dcache` command: a small local helper for improving **DeepSeek** prompt-cache hit rate in opencode, Claude Code, and Codex CLI. It starts a Web report, installs managed hooks/commands, and can route model traffic through a local proxy so cache hits are measured from real DeepSeek responses.

It is not a general cache system for every model. The tests, reports, and defaults are tuned for DeepSeek cache behavior.

This project was inspired by the cache-stability idea in https://github.com/esengine/DeepSeek-Reasonix. dcache is a separate implementation.

The effect is visible immediately in the latest verified workspace run:

| CLI route | Baseline cache hit ratio | With dcache / anchor | 
|---|---|---|
| Codex CLI + real DeepSeek V4 Flash | `0.1399` | `0.9921`hot case-first /`0.9921`cross-session first requests | 
| opencode + real DeepSeek V4 Flash | `0.2098` | `0.9916`anchor hot requests | 
| Claude Code + real DeepSeek Anthropic API | `0%`case-first | `100%`hot case-first /`100%`cross-session first requests | 

Verification snapshot: `npm run verify` passed typecheck, 49 tests, and build on Node.js `v22.18.0`.

Install from the published package name:

```
npm install -g dcache-proxy
dcache serve --project /path/to/project
```
For a source checkout:

```
npm install
npm run build
npm install -g .
dcache serve --project /path/to/project
```
Open `http://127.0.0.1:48731`.

If the default ports are busy, `dcache serve` automatically picks free ports and prints the actual Web/proxy URLs.

To launch the sidecar in the background:

`dcache serve --project /path/to/project --daemon`In the Web page, use the simple buttons:

- **Hook opencode / Claude / Codex**: install managed listeners and the automatic workspace- `newanchor`helper.
- **Hook ... + connect API**: also route that CLI through the local proxy, so reports show real DeepSeek cache hits.
- **Uninstall ...**: remove only files marked as managed by dcache.

CLI examples:

```
dcache hook --project /path/to/project
dcache hook --target opencode --connect-api --project /path/to/project
dcache hook --target claude --connect-api --project /path/to/project
dcache hook --target codex --connect-api --project /path/to/project
dcache hook --target all --connect-api --project /path/to/project
dcache hook --auto --connect-api --project /path/to/project
dcache uninstall --target all --project /path/to/project
```
`dcache hook --auto` scans common opencode, Claude Code, and Codex config locations and installs into every detected supported tool. For Codex, discovery may use an existing profile/config as a signal, but installation writes hook config to `$CODEX_HOME/hooks.json`.

For Codex, dcache creates a `dcache` profile. Run Codex with that profile:

```
codex -p dcache -m deepseek-v4-flash
codex -p dcache -m deepseek-v4-flash exec "your prompt"
```
Set `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` in your shell before using the connected route.

- opencode plugin: `.opencode/plugins/dcache.js`
- Claude Code hook: `.claude/hooks/dcache.mjs`
- Codex hook: `.codex/hooks/dcache.mjs`
- opencode/Claude `/newanchor`command and Codex`$newanchor`skill
- local data and logs under `.dcache/`
- for Codex only: `$CODEX_HOME/hooks.json`and`$CODEX_HOME/dcache.config.toml`

It does not overwrite user-owned files with the same path. Uninstall removes only dcache-managed files.

Anchor is **off by default**.

Turn it on when the project is small or stable and you expect many related conversations. dcache now detects the current workspace every session and creates the anchor automatically:

- Git workspaces use a repository identity, preferring `remote.origin.url`when available.
- SVN workspaces use repository UUID/URL when available.
- Non-VCS folders use a normalized path-derived identity.

Each workspace gets its own slot under `.dcache/anchors/<workspace-id>/`, so multiple anchors can coexist. Switching to another workspace selects that workspace's anchor and does not mutate the current one.

The workspace id is designed to be stable across Windows, macOS, and Linux when the clones point at the same Git `remote.origin.url`. That means one logical anchor can be reused from different terminals or OSes for the same repository, helping DeepSeek hit the same prompt-cache prefix across machines.

Recommended setup:

- 
Use the same canonical Git origin on every machine/clone, for example `https://github.com/org/repo.git`. If one clone uses SSH and another uses HTTPS, normalize them first with`git remote set-url origin ...`.
- 
Install and run dcache for the local clone on each OS: dcache serve --project /path/to/repo dcache hook --target all --connect-api --project /path/to/repo 
- 
Enable the anchor once per clone/tool with the Web toggle, `/newanchor on`, or`$newanchor on`in Codex.
- 
For the most byte-identical anchor metadata across multiple local clones or machines, put the dcache data directory in a synced/shared location and pass the same logical data dir to both `serve`and`hook`on that machine:dcache serve --project /path/to/repo --data-dir /path/to/shared/dcache/repo dcache hook --target all --connect-api --project /path/to/repo --data-dir /path/to/shared/dcache/repo 

Notes:

- The DeepSeek cache is provider-side, so all terminals should use the same upstream/model and compatible API credentials.
- Do not use different custom anchor text per OS if you want cross-platform cache hits.
- If there is no Git remote, dcache falls back to local git/path identity; separate OS paths will not be treated as one shared Git identity.

Tradeoffs:

- good for small/stable projects and repeated work;
- costs extra prompt tokens;
- stale anchor state can bias answers if you force custom text;
- never put secrets in optional custom anchor text;
- usually unnecessary for one-off prompts or fast-changing context.

Controls:

```
/newanchor status
/newanchor on
/newanchor off
/newanchor reset
```
Use `$newanchor` in Codex. The Web page has the same toggle/reset buttons and shows the anchor change log. Passing text after `on` is still accepted as a legacy override, but normal use is just the on/off button.

dcache resets anchor generation for current-workspace context changes such as opencode `/new`, forks, Claude `/clear`, and compact/resume-like Codex events. A brand-new terminal window does not reset anchor just because the window is new; a different workspace uses a different anchor slot.

The Web report shows:

- DeepSeek cache hit ratio;
- recent model requests;
- opencode, Claude Code, and Codex status;
- anchor enabled/off state;
- anchor changes and reset history;
- warnings such as prefix drift or missing cache usage.

- **opencode**gives the cleanest session/fork signals.
- **Claude Code**may send extra internal requests. For fair comparisons, dcache looks at case-first requests as well as totals.
- **Codex CLI**uses the OpenAI Responses API shape. dcache translates it to DeepSeek Chat Completions at the local proxy. Codex hook execution depends on the local Codex hooks feature/trust mode, so dcache also injects the anchor in the proxy path when anchor is enabled.
- **connect API**requires the sidecar to be running. If it is stopped, the connected CLI may fail model calls until you restart dcache or uninstall the route.
- Keep Web/API/proxy ports on localhost or a trusted network.

The real tests use the same business prompts where possible:

- shared prompts: `tests/dcache-real-cases.ts`
- opencode E2E: `tests/dcache-local-opencode.test.ts`
- Claude Code E2E: `tests/dcache-local-claude.test.ts`
- Codex CLI E2E: `tests/dcache-local-codex.test.ts`
- anchor behavior: `tests/dcache-anchor.test.ts`
- proxy feature regression tests: `tests/dcache-proxy-features.test.ts`

The headline results are shown near the top under **Tested impact**. This section keeps the test files and reproduction commands together.

For Codex/opencode/Claude, the first anchored request may be a warm-up. The important cross-session signal is the next first request of a new case/session.

Optional real test commands:

```
$env:OPENCODE_REAL_E2E="1"
$env:OPENCODE_REAL_MODEL="deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash"
npm run test:e2e:opencode
$env:CLAUDE_REAL_E2E="1"
$env:CLAUDE_REAL_MODEL="haiku"
npm run test:e2e:claude
$env:CODEX_REAL_E2E="1"
$env:CODEX_REAL_MODEL="deepseek-v4-flash"
npx vitest run tests/dcache-local-codex.test.ts --testNamePattern "real DeepSeek"
```
Docker files are kept for source-checkout/self-hosting workflows, but they are not included in the npm package.

`docker compose up --build`The compose file uses `restart: unless-stopped`.

If host ports are occupied:

`DCACHE_WEB_PORT=48732 DCACHE_PROXY_PORT=11489 docker compose up --build`Target runtime: Node.js `>=18.0.0`. The project is designed for Windows, macOS, and Linux.

`dcache-proxy` 提供 `dcache` 命令：这是一个本地小工具，专门优化 **DeepSeek** 的 prompt cache 命中率，面向 opencode、Claude Code 和 Codex CLI。它会启动 Web 报表，安装托管的 hook/命令，也可以把模型请求转到本地代理，从真实 DeepSeek 响应里统计缓存命中。

它不是所有模型通用的缓存系统；测试、报表和默认逻辑都围绕 DeepSeek 的缓存行为设计。

本项目参考了 https://github.com/esengine/DeepSeek-Reasonix 的缓存稳定思路，但实现是独立的。

当前工作区最新验证里，dcache 能把真实 DeepSeek 请求的缓存命中率稳定拉到接近满命中：

| CLI 路由 | 不开插件 baseline | 使用 dcache / anchor 后 | 
|---|---|---|
| Codex CLI + 真实 DeepSeek V4 Flash | `0.1399` | `0.9921`hot case-first /`0.9921`跨 session first requests | 
| opencode + 真实 DeepSeek V4 Flash | `0.2098` | `0.9916`anchor 热请求 | 
| Claude Code + 真实 DeepSeek Anthropic API | `0%`case-first | `100%`hot case-first /`100%`跨 session first requests | 

验证快照：`npm run verify` 在 Node.js `v22.18.0` 上通过了类型检查、49 个测试和构建。

从发布包安装：

```
npm install -g dcache-proxy
dcache serve --project /path/to/project
```
从源码仓库安装：

```
npm install
npm run build
npm install -g .
dcache serve --project /path/to/project
```
打开 `http://127.0.0.1:48731`。

默认端口被占用时，`dcache serve` 会自动换空闲端口，并打印实际 Web/proxy 地址。

后台启动 sidecar：

`dcache serve --project /path/to/project --daemon`Web 页面里可以直接点按钮：

- **Hook opencode / Claude / Codex**：安装监听和自动 workspace- `newanchor`辅助命令。
- **Hook ... + connect API**：同时让该 CLI 走本地代理，报表才能显示真实 DeepSeek 缓存命中。
- **Uninstall ...**：只删除 dcache 托管标记的文件。

命令行例子：

```
dcache hook --project /path/to/project
dcache hook --target opencode --connect-api --project /path/to/project
dcache hook --target claude --connect-api --project /path/to/project
dcache hook --target codex --connect-api --project /path/to/project
dcache hook --target all --connect-api --project /path/to/project
dcache hook --auto --connect-api --project /path/to/project
dcache uninstall --target all --project /path/to/project
```
`dcache hook --auto` 会扫描常见的 opencode、Claude Code 和 Codex 配置位置，并给检测到的受支持工具自动安装。Codex 的发现可以通过已有 profile/config 判断，但安装 hook 时会写入 `$CODEX_HOME/hooks.json`。

Codex 会生成 `dcache` profile，使用时这样启动：

```
codex -p dcache -m deepseek-v4-flash
codex -p dcache -m deepseek-v4-flash exec "你的 prompt"
```
使用 connect API 前，在 shell 里配置 `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY`。

- opencode 插件：`.opencode/plugins/dcache.js`
- Claude Code hook：`.claude/hooks/dcache.mjs`
- Codex hook：`.codex/hooks/dcache.mjs`
- opencode/Claude 的 `/newanchor`命令，以及 Codex 的`$newanchor`skill
- `.dcache/`下的本地数据和日志
- 仅 Codex：`$CODEX_HOME/hooks.json`和`$CODEX_HOME/dcache.config.toml`

不会覆盖同路径的用户自有文件。卸载时只删除 dcache 托管的文件。

Anchor **默认关闭**。

适合在小项目、稳定项目、需要连续多轮相关对话时开启。dcache 会在每次 session 自动识别当前工作区并生成 anchor：

- Git 工作区优先使用 `remote.origin.url`，没有远端时退回 git 目录身份。
- SVN 工作区优先使用仓库 UUID/URL。
- 非 VCS 目录使用规范化路径派生的身份。

每个工作区都有独立槽位：`.dcache/anchors/<workspace-id>/`。因此多个 anchor 可以同时存在；切到另一个工作区只会使用那个工作区的 anchor，不会改动当前工作区。

只要不同机器/终端的 clone 指向同一个 Git `remote.origin.url`，workspace id 会保持稳定；因此同一个仓库可以在 Windows、macOS、Linux 上复用同一个逻辑 anchor，让 DeepSeek 更容易命中同一段 prompt-cache 前缀。

推荐配置：

- 
每个系统上的仓库使用同一个规范化 origin，例如 `https://github.com/org/repo.git`。如果有的机器用 SSH、有的机器用 HTTPS，先统一：git remote set-url origin https://github.com/org/repo.git 
- 
在每个系统的本地 clone 上安装并启动 dcache： dcache serve --project /path/to/repo dcache hook --target all --connect-api --project /path/to/repo 
- 
用 Web 开关、 `/newanchor on`，或 Codex 里的`$newanchor on`打开 anchor。
- 
如果你希望多个本地 clone 或多台机器上的 anchor 元数据也尽量字节一致，可以把 dcache 数据目录放到同步盘/共享目录，并且 `serve`和`hook`都显式使用同一个逻辑`--data-dir`：dcache serve --project /path/to/repo --data-dir /path/to/shared/dcache/repo dcache hook --target all --connect-api --project /path/to/repo --data-dir /path/to/shared/dcache/repo 

注意：

- DeepSeek prompt cache 是服务端缓存；跨平台命中时，各终端应使用同一个上游、模型和兼容的 API 凭据。
- 想跨平台命中时，不要在不同 OS 写入不同的自定义 anchor 文本。
- 如果仓库没有 Git remote，dcache 会退回本地 git/path 身份；不同系统路径不会自动视为同一个 Git 身份。

取舍：

- 小而稳定的项目可以常开；
- 会增加 prompt token；
- 如果强制写入自定义文本，过期内容会影响回答；
- 不要把密钥写进可选的自定义 anchor 文本；
- 一次性短任务通常不需要。

控制方式：

```
/newanchor status
/newanchor on
/newanchor off
/newanchor reset
```
Codex 里用 `$newanchor`。Web 页面也有同样的开关/重置按钮，并会展示 anchor 变动日志。`on` 后面继续带文本仍兼容为旧式覆盖，但正常用法只需要开关。

dcache 会在当前工作区真实上下文变化时重置 anchor generation，例如 opencode `/new`、fork、Claude `/clear`、Codex compact/resume 类事件。单纯新开终端窗口不会因为“新窗口”而重置；不同工作区会进入不同 anchor 槽位。

Web 报表会展示：

- DeepSeek 缓存命中率；
- 最近模型请求；
- opencode、Claude Code、Codex 状态；
- anchor 开关状态；
- anchor 变动和 reset 历史；
- prefix drift、缺少缓存用量等警告。

- **opencode**的 session/fork 信号最清晰。
- **Claude Code**可能会发送额外内部请求，所以公平对比时除了总量，也看每个用例的第一条请求。
- **Codex CLI**使用 OpenAI Responses API 形态。dcache 会在本地代理里转换成 DeepSeek Chat Completions。Codex hook 是否执行受本地 hooks 功能和信任设置影响，所以 anchor 开启时，dcache 也会在代理路径注入 anchor，保证缓存优化不完全依赖 hook 是否触发。
- **connect API**需要 sidecar 一直运行。如果 sidecar 停了，已连接到本地代理的 CLI 可能无法请求模型，直到重启 dcache 或卸载路由。
- Web/API/proxy 端口建议只暴露在本机或可信内网。

真实测试尽量使用同一组业务 prompt：

- 共享用例：`tests/dcache-real-cases.ts`
- opencode E2E：`tests/dcache-local-opencode.test.ts`
- Claude Code E2E：`tests/dcache-local-claude.test.ts`
- Codex CLI E2E：`tests/dcache-local-codex.test.ts`
- anchor 行为：`tests/dcache-anchor.test.ts`
- proxy 功能回归：`tests/dcache-proxy-features.test.ts`

前面的“测试效果”已经展示当前工作区最新验证结果；这里保留用例和复现命令。

Codex/opencode/Claude 的第一条 anchor 请求可能只是预热；更关键的是下一条新 case/session 的 first request。

可选真实测试命令：

```
$env:OPENCODE_REAL_E2E="1"
$env:OPENCODE_REAL_MODEL="deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash"
npm run test:e2e:opencode
$env:CLAUDE_REAL_E2E="1"
$env:CLAUDE_REAL_MODEL="haiku"
npm run test:e2e:claude
$env:CODEX_REAL_E2E="1"
$env:CODEX_REAL_MODEL="deepseek-v4-flash"
npx vitest run tests/dcache-local-codex.test.ts --testNamePattern "real DeepSeek"
```
Docker 文件保留给源码仓库和自托管场景使用，但不会包含在 npm 发布包里。

`docker compose up --build`Compose 使用 `restart: unless-stopped`。

宿主机端口被占用时：

`DCACHE_WEB_PORT=48732 DCACHE_PROXY_PORT=11489 docker compose up --build`目标运行时：Node.js `>=18.0.0`。项目面向 Windows、macOS 和 Linux。

title: GitHub - rohaquinlop/pi-deepseek-cache: Optimize DeepSeek prefix cache hit rates — reduce API input costs by 95%+ in multi-turn coding sessions · GitHub
markdown:
---
title: GitHub - rohaquinlop/pi-deepseek-cache: Optimize DeepSeek prefix cache hit rates — reduce API input costs by 95%+ in multi-turn coding sessions
author: Rohaquinlop
url: https://github.com/rohaquinlop/pi-deepseek-cache
hostname: github.com
description: Optimize DeepSeek prefix cache hit rates — reduce API input costs by 95%+ in multi-turn coding sessions - rohaquinlop/pi-deepseek-cache
sitename: GitHub
categories: ['repository:1275516531']
---
**Reduce DeepSeek API costs by 95%+** through multi-layered prefix cache optimization. Zero configuration — auto-detects DeepSeek models and applies best practices transparently.

DeepSeek's API uses prefix caching — identical prompt prefixes served from disk cache at **50–120× lower cost** than fresh computation. But the cache only works when every byte from position 0 is identical across requests.

Pi's default system prompt embeds `Current date: YYYY-MM-DD` and `Current working directory: <cwd>` — dynamic values that change daily and per session, silently busting the entire prefix cache.

| Layer | Feature | Impact | 
|---|---|---|
| P0 | Date & CWD freeze | Root-cause fix — locks session date and directory, preventing daily/per-session cache bust | 
| P1 | Hit-rate telemetry | Per-session hit rate shown as dimmed footer status line; /cache-stats & /cache-graph for detail | 
| P2 | Prefix guard | SHA-256 hash diagnostics — tracks prefix breaks (viewable in /cache-stats) | 
| P3 | Cache-friendly compaction | Deterministic summarization via deepseek-v4-flash at temperature 0, SHA-256 cached for stable replays | 
| P4 | TUI overlays | `/cache-stats`popup with hit rate, tokens, cost savings.`/cache-graph`ASCII trend chart | 

| Without Extension | With Extension | |
|---|---|---|
| deepseek-v4-flashinput | $0.14/M tokens | $0.003/M tokens (98% less) | 
| deepseek-v4-proinput | $3.00/M tokens | $0.025/M tokens (99% less) | 

`pi install npm:@rohaquinlop/pi-deepseek-cache`Or via git:

`pi install git:github.com/rohaquinlop/pi-deepseek-cache`The extension activates automatically. No configuration needed. The per-session
cache hit rate appears as a dimmed status line (`Cache 96.2%`) in Pi's footer.
Pi's native `CH:XX.X%` shows the per-turn rate in the stats line. Detailed
stats are available via /cache-stats and /cache-graph commands.

Each pi session writes its own `stats-{sessionId}.json` and
`history-{sessionId}.json` files, so concurrent sessions never race on the
same file. Session files older than 30 days are cleaned up automatically.

Works with any provider serving DeepSeek models:

- **Any provider**with- `deepseek-*`model IDs (NaN Builders, OpenRouter, custom proxies, etc.)
- **DeepSeek API**(- `deepseek`provider) — direct API users

Non-DeepSeek models pass through unchanged.

This extension automatically applies to subagent processes that use DeepSeek
models. It declares `appliesToModels: ["deepseek-*", "deepseek"]` in its
`package.json`, which the pi-subagents
extension detects and loads into child processes — no configuration needed.

For the best cache performance, ensure both extensions are installed:

```
pi install npm:@rohaquinlop/pi-subagents
pi install npm:@rohaquinlop/pi-deepseek-cache
```
Overlay popup showing two sections: **this session's** stats and an **aggregate across all sessions** (N sessions). Each section shows hit rate, cache read/write/input tokens, turns, and estimated cost savings.

ASCII trend chart of hit rate over turns — helps spot regressions.

Clears all cached statistics, history, and summary cache — deletes all per-session `stats-*.json` and `history-*.json` files plus the summary cache. Useful after major prompt changes.

**P0 (Date/CWD freeze):** On `before_agent_start`, replaces the dynamic `Current date` and `Current working directory` lines with values frozen at session start. The system prompt prefix stays byte-identical across the entire session.

**P1 (Telemetry):** Accumulates `cacheRead`, `input`, `cacheWrite`, and `turns` from every assistant message's usage data. Each session stores its stats in `stats-{sessionId}.json` so concurrent sessions never race. `/cache-stats` shows both this session's stats and an aggregate across all sessions.

**P2 (Prefix guard):** On `before_provider_request`, SHA-256 hashes all messages except the last to fingerprint the prefix. Tracks when the hash changes — the break count is visible in `/cache-stats`.

**P3 (Compaction):** On `session_before_compact`, summarizes conversation history with deepseek-v4-flash at temperature 0. Summaries are SHA-256 hashed and cached — identical histories produce byte-identical summaries, keeping compaction cache-stable.

**P4 (Overlays):** `/cache-stats` and `/cache-graph` render as TUI overlay popups (Esc to dismiss) with formatted hit-rate data and ASCII trend charts.

MIT

I'm not sure you need a "DeepSeek native coding agent" to take advantage of DeepSeeks cache, yesterday as the Codex quota usage issue still wasn't solved for me, I wrote a tiny little bridge so I could use DeepSeek V4 Pro via Codex, and seems most of everything I did was basically cached as far as I can tell: https://i.imgur.com/7eKn6wN.png (2026-05-23 Input (Cache hit): 39,123,200 tokens, Input (Cache miss) 1,692,286), and the bridge is doing not special, just massage the DeepSeek API shape into what Codex expects, nothing particular about caching at all.

Besides being even better at the caching, I'm not sure what benefits you'd get compared to just firing up OpenCode with the DeepSeek API yourself, it'll similarly do caching for sure and also "talks directly to api.deepseek.com" if that matters, and you'll get a much more mature harness.

They explain some of the the reasons why they have a better solution and why they are very opinionated

>Automatic prefix caching activates only when the exact byte prefix of the previous request matches. Most agent loops reorder, rewrite, or inject fresh timestamps each turn — cache hit rate in practice: <20%.

So they optimize on this plus other techniques to improve cache hits, making it cheaper.

The last time I heard about something like this, it was Claude Code intentionally injecting random strings to break caching when you're not using a Claude model. Aside from that kind of intentional sabotage, I don't think any coding agent would just ignore prefix caching.

I'm not sure what the mechanism is, but I've definitely had Claude refuse to work on sessions that were touched by other models. Some kind of integrity check failure. Resetting the session back to the point before I used the other model fixed the problem.

IIRC Anthropic's API produces cryptographic signatures for thinking blocks. If you try to submit a set of messages that include thinking blocks with missing/invalid signatures, it'll refuse.

They do this to mitigate jailbreak attempts that rely on fabricated message history (e.g. making it look like the model was compliant in previous messages, increasing the likelihood that it'll continue to be compliant in future messages).

>Most agent loops reorder, rewrite, or inject fresh timestamps each turn

That's really surprising, since it'd defeat the whole point of KV caching. I mean I buy it considering how sloppily coded the harnesses seem to be, but this like obvious low hanging fruit.

I've also often wondered why LLMs aren't trained with a format of having a dedicated contextual system-instruction role at the _end_, which you could use to put context like current time or other misc stuff.

There are context pruning strategies that will prune old messages that are no longer relevant, and context compaction from summaries, etc. But to say "most" do this on "every turn" is overstating things. I think it's more correct to say that "many" do this "occasionally."

I'm also not sure what they mean about injecting fresh timestamps. I could see why you'd prepend/append a timestamp to the user's messages to make the model aware of the current time, and the passage of time, but I can't think of any good reason to edit timestamps in prior messages. I'm sure someone can come up with one, but I'd be very surprised if this was a thing that most agent loops do, let along doing it on every turn.

i put together this, for myself so i can try to track what coding agents are doing, I add agents to it or topics (like caching, or sandboxing, file editing methods, etc) just to try and find anything novel or good, since I am/was considering making a new harness but using all the best things from any of those. > Most agent loops reorder, rewrite, or inject fresh timestamps each turn

I haven't seen that, it'd be crazy slow if they did this. What "agent loops" are they talking about here specifically? The vagueness makes it sound potentially made up.

I've never seen an agent loop "reorder, rewrite, or inject fresh timestamps" each turn other than mostly towards the end of the messages. Messing with a large part of the context every turn would be a fairly crazy thing to do.

It's a really lazy one too - there are so many open source harnesses, including e.g. Codex and Kimi-CLI, and of course the leaked Claude Code source, so it's trivial to verify if someone even just bothered to ask an agent to check actual source code examples.

> tool call pruning breaks cache and people will tell you this is horrible and expensive

> except i looked at some anthropic data and real user behavior ends up with better cache hits and 30% less spend

> even this is needs to be analyzed further, it's just not simple

> for openai data it's inverted! cache hit ratio is actually better [sic: I think he meant worse based on the screenshot] with tool call pruning turned on

> kimi is a funny one - it has better cache hits with pruning on...but is also more expensive!

There was also another thread recently where he discussed that pruning improves user experience (models are smarter with less context) but I can't find it.

My understanding of caching with most models/providers is that a prefix substring of the context has to be reused for a cache hit, but not necessarily the whole entire context window. So if you prune tool calls from the history, you're going to get one cache miss on the newly-pruned history, and then you're going to be getting cache hits on every subsequent turn, with a lower number of input tokens. If you prune subsequent tool calls after that, you would still get a cache hit for the already-pruned portion of the context, just not the full context.

The way you do this (and the way opencode does it) is you do most of your pruning in more recent history. Last I looked at opencode, they start pruning tool call results after 2 full agentic turns. So you probably dont get quite as good hits on cache for the most recent 1-5% of your turns, but after that everything else caches fine and those tool calls that likely aren't relavent to your session anymore are gone.

> our implementation is it only prunes calls from > 3 user messages ago, if context is > 40K, and only if there's at least 20K tokens to be removed

Seems reasonable to me and explains why I can have long sessions (way longer than with zed agents) while still hitting cache. Opencode is just missing per-provider TTL.

I found that keeping current context utilization at 18% of total context length was best for minimizing spend, across all models with 400k context length or more

I can't confirm this. Having utilized Opencode for a large project over the past 10 months, with multiple models and agents, we've never run into such 'cache stability issues'."

> Ah, reminds me of good old "There are only 2 hard problems in computer science: cache invalidation, naming things, and off-by-1 errors."

You quip, but LLM KV caching (from the harness side) is quite easy: You get a cache hit on stable prompt prefixes, period. That means you want to keep the prefix stable, and only append at the end of the conversation.
Made up example: Don't put the git branch name into the system prompt part (that comes first), as whenever the branch name changes, that'd trigger a cache invalidation of the entire prompt.

Getting this right requires some care to not by accident modify the prefix, basically, and some design on communicating the things that can change (user configuration, working dir, git information, ...).

That sounds like the experience of writing Containerfiles; since steps are cached you want to pull the thing you are iterating on as far down as possible.

All of this work has been done before in different contexts. Memory management with bigger blocks and weaker definitions that change whenever some grad student gets a bright idea.

100%. Since you mention memory management: Generational GC is pretty much the same idea: Keep the stuff that's least likely to change an important property (liveness) together.

Conceptually the underlying general idea is to sort things based on stability if you can avoid recomputing properties of the stable part.

What I noticed when using OpenCode with llama.cpp, was that the default host RAM prompt cache size in llama.cpp was way too small for say 128k Qwen3.6 27B.

How can you have cache hit efficiency? Isn't it just a matter of not changing the previous context? I don't understand what knobs there are to tweak on this.

> Isn't it just a matter of not changing the previous context?

Yes, but a lot of harnesses change previous context. E.g. the system prompt injects the current time/date, working directory, files in the working directory, etc. Compaction also changes the whole previous context. I _think_ changing the list of tools also invalidates cache, so invoking a subagent with different tools would invalidate the cache.

My vague impression is that it's in a similar vein to functional programming languages. It generally disallows doing things that lead to bugs (cache misses in this case), and presumably allows you to do those things in a way that makes it much clearer that this is likely to cause cache misses. I would guess that in this paradigm, you don't mutate your existing session, you derive a new session by mutating the prior context into a new context.

Cache is always there, it’s just that it only caches up to the point where an input token changes. So if the tools list is early in the prompt, changing it would limit cache for most of the prompt. If the tools list is the last thing, you could still get 99% cache hits even if it changes every turn.

After a couple of turns the system prompt is a small part of the context. Not changing the system prompt at all is key so that the rest of the history is itself part of the prefix.

Depends upon the service and how the harness is built, Some of the services allow for very few cache keys, so you won't necessarily get any cache if you edit recent messages as the cache is not per message, but big blocks of everything up to a cache key.

This was actually surprising to me when I learned about it as I have never worked with (or built) any cache working like that before.
